flashcards.front: Define Computer Vision in the context of Deep Learning and list its primary tasks.
flashcards.back: Computer Vision in DL enables machines to interpret visual data using neural networks (primarily CNNs) that learn hierarchical features automatically. Primary tasks include: Image Classification (labeling entire images), Object Detection (localizing objects with bounding boxes), Image Segmentation (pixel-level classification), and Image Captioning (generating textual descriptions). These replace traditional hand-crafted feature extraction with data-driven learning.

flashcards.front: Compare YOLO (You Only Look Once) and Faster R-CNN for object detection.
flashcards.back: YOLO is a single-shot detector performing detection in one forward pass (real-time at 30-60 FPS), suitable for applications like autonomous driving. Faster R-CNN is a two-stage detector using region proposal networks (~2000 proposals), offering higher accuracy but slower processing. For real-time systems, YOLO is preferred; for precision-critical tasks like medical imaging, Faster R-CNN variants excel. The trade-off is between speed and mAP (mean Average Precision).

flashcards.front: Differentiate between Semantic Segmentation and Instance Segmentation.
flashcards.back: Semantic Segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel (all 'car' pixels labeled 'car'), providing scene understanding. Instance Segmentation distinguishes individual objects of the same class (separate masks for each car). Semantic segmentation uses architectures like U-Net and FCN; Instance Segmentation uses Mask R-CNN (adds mask prediction branch to Faster R-CNN). Autonomous driving requires instance segmentation to distinguish nearby vehicles.

flashcards.front: Explain the components of a modern Face Recognition system.
flashcards.back: Modern face recognition involves: (1) Face Detection - locating faces (MTCNN, RetinaFace); (2) Face Alignment - normalizing landmarks; (3) Feature Extraction - encoding to embedding vector using CNNs (FaceNet, ArcFace); (4) Face Matching - comparing embeddings via cosine similarity. ArcFace uses additive angular margin loss to enhance intra-class compactness and inter-class disparity, achieving >99% accuracy on LFW benchmark, surpassing human performance.

flashcards.front: Describe Neural Style Transfer and its loss functions.
flashcards.back: Neural Style Transfer creates artistic images by combining content from one image with style from another using pretrained CNNs (typically VGG-19). It optimizes a generated image to minimize three losses: (1) Content Loss - maintains spatial structure (Euclidean distance between feature maps); (2) Style Loss - matches Gram matrices capturing texture patterns; (3) Total Variation Loss - ensures spatial smoothness. Applications include photo editing apps, creative AI tools, and texture synthesis.

ResNet introduced skip (residual) connections enabling training of very deep networks (50-152 layers) by mitigating vanishing gradients: output = F(x) + x. EfficientNet uses compound scaling (uniformly scaling depth, width, resolution) achieving better efficiency. EfficientNet-B0 (5.3M params) outperforms ResNet-50 (25.6M params) on ImageNet with 5x fewer parameters. For mobile/edge deployment, EfficientNet is preferred; ResNet remains popular for transfer learning due to extensive pretrained weights.

flashcards.front: What is Transfer Learning in Computer Vision, and how is it implemented?
flashcards.back: Transfer Learning adapts pretrained models (trained on ImageNet's 14M images) to new tasks with limited data. Implementation strategies: (1) Feature Extraction - freeze pretrained layers, train new classifier; (2) Fine-tuning - unfreeze top layers with low learning rate; (3) Domain Adaptation - adjust for different distributions. This reduces training time dramatically and achieves high accuracy with small datasets, essential for practical CV applications in medical, industrial, and retail domains.

flashcards.front: Explain the U-Net architecture and its applications in medical imaging.
flashcards.back: U-Net is an encoder-decoder architecture (symmetric FCN) with skip connections, designed for biomedical image segmentation. The encoder (contracting path) captures context; the decoder (expanding path) enables precise localization. Skip connections concatenate features from encoder to decoder, preserving spatial information. U-Net achieves state-of-the-art results in tumor segmentation, retinal vessel detection, and cell counting from microscopy images, outperforming traditional methods in accuracy and efficiency.
